objections.description: The artifact is truncated mid-sentence in the contrast compliance matrix. The entire section cuts off at '--text-link #8B7' — meaning the contrast verification for link colors, error/success/warning semantic colors on their muted backgrounds, and the entire light theme contrast matrix are missing. Without verified contrast ratios for ALL token pairings in BOTH themes, this spec cannot claim WCAG 2.1 AA compliance. This is a shipping blocker: you're telling engineers 'these tokens are accessible' but haven't actually proven it for ~40% of the color combinations.
objections.suggested_fix: Complete the contrast matrix for both dark and light themes covering: text-link on all backgrounds, text-primary/secondary on surface-selected/surface-critical/accent-muted, semantic colors (success/error/warning) on their muted backgrounds, and inverse text on accent. Also add the PROHIBITED usage rules for tertiary text on elevated surfaces (which was started but not completed).

objections.location: Section 1.5 - Component Architecture Map / API layer
objections.description: The api.js file is listed in the directory structure but never specified. There's no API client implementation, no error handling strategy for network failures, no retry policy, no request cancellation on unmount, and no typing of API response shapes. The useApiRequest hook is referenced in the state ownership table but never defined. Engineers will each invent their own fetch wrapper, leading to inconsistent error handling across features — exactly the kind of 'wrong abstraction through duplication' that kills you at scale.
objections.suggested_fix: Define api.js with: (1) a base fetch wrapper that handles JSON parsing, non-2xx → thrown errors with status codes, and AbortController integration, (2) the useApiRequest hook implementation with loading/error/data states and automatic abort on unmount, (3) explicit error type definitions (NetworkError vs ApiError with status), (4) debounce strategy for autocomplete/suggest endpoints specifically. Show the hook contract so feature developers know exactly what they get.

objections.description: Search query lives as local state in SearchScreen, but there's no specification for URL synchronization. If a user searches for 'contract renewal', the URL should be /search?q=contract+renewal so the result is shareable and back-button works correctly. The current spec routes to /search with no query parameter handling. This is a UX regression from even the simplest search implementation and will require a painful retrofit if not designed in now.
objections.suggested_fix: Add query parameter syncing: SearchScreen reads initial query from URL search params via wouter's useSearch or window.location.search, and updates the URL (replaceState, not pushState for typing; pushState on submit) as the user types/submits. Define the exact URL shape: /search?q=encoded_query. This also means the SPA fallback in web_server.py correctly passes query strings through — verify that the FileResponse approach preserves query params (it does, since the browser handles them client-side, but state it explicitly).

objections.location: Section 2.1 - SPA fallback via 404 exception handler
objections.description: Overriding the global 404 exception handler is dangerous. Any future developer adding a new API route that accidentally 404s (e.g., a typo in a path parameter handler) will get index.html served to API consumers if they happen to send Accept: text/html (which Postman and some HTTP clients do by default). The Accept header content negotiation is a reasonable heuristic but not bulletproof. More critically, this overrides ALL StarletteHTTPException handling — a 403 or 500 from an API endpoint would lose its original status code if someone throws HTTPException(403) and the handler only checks for 404.

The motion system is described conceptually (slide left/right, crossfade, ActionSheet slides up) but the actual CSS animations are not defined anywhere in this spec. Section references 'animations.css' in the file tree but provides zero implementation. The convergence criteria explicitly require motion behavior approval. What are the exact durations? What easing curves (given that ease/linear are banned)? What are the CSS class names and keyframes? Without this, every agent is approving a promise, not a spec.
objections.suggested_fix: Add a Motion System section with: (1) the specific cubic-bezier curves used (e.g., cubic-bezier(0.32, 0.72, 0, 1) for Flighty-style entries), (2) duration tokens as CSS custom properties (--duration-fast: 150ms, --duration-normal: 250ms, --duration-slow: 400ms), (3) the actual @keyframes for slide-in-right, slide-out-left, slide-up (ActionSheet), fade-in, fade-out, (4) prefers-reduced-motion handling that collapses all animations to instant/opacity-only, (5) the CSS class application logic in AnimatedRoute.

objections.description: Typography tokens are completely missing. For a spec that claims Nubank+Flighty premium aesthetic, there are zero font-family, font-size, line-height, letter-spacing, or font-weight tokens. Tailwind utility classes alone don't enforce consistency — two engineers will pick text-sm vs text-xs for secondary labels and you'll get visual inconsistency across screens. Spacing scale is also absent.
objections.suggested_fix: Add typography tokens: --font-sans (system stack or specific typeface), --font-mono. Define a type scale with semantic names: --text-xs through --text-3xl with corresponding line-heights and letter-spacing. Define font-weight tokens: --weight-normal (400), --weight-medium (500), --weight-semibold (600), --weight-bold (700). Define spacing tokens if deviating from Tailwind defaults, or explicitly state 'spacing uses Tailwind's default 4px scale with no custom tokens'.

objections.description: Skeleton is listed as the Suspense fallback instead of spinners (good), but there's no specification of what the skeleton looks like per screen. A generic pulsing rectangle is barely better than a spinner. Each lazy-loaded screen needs a specific skeleton layout that matches its content structure to prevent CLS. The Skeleton component's variant prop ('text', 'card', 'circle', 'block') is too generic — it needs screen-specific skeleton compositions.
objections.suggested_fix: Define skeleton compositions per screen: DashboardSkeleton (stat bars + card grid), SearchSkeleton (input + result list), DocumentSkeleton (header + timeline entries). These are the actual Suspense fallback components. The base Skeleton primitive is fine for composing them, but the compositions must be specified to prevent CLS — each needs explicit height/width matching the real content's dimensions.

suggestions.description: BaseHTTPMiddleware has known performance issues in Starlette (it wraps every request in a thread). Since this middleware only needs to add a header to /assets/ paths, use a pure ASGI middleware or a simpler approach: configure StaticFiles with a custom headers parameter, or use Starlette's middleware stack with a lightweight implementation. At low traffic this doesn't matter, but it's worth noting for 10x scale.

The FOUC prevention script in index.html is mentioned in Section 3.8 but Section 3.8 doesn't exist in this artifact. Include the actual inline script: it should read localStorage, set data-theme on documentElement synchronously before React hydrates. Without seeing it, we can't verify it handles the edge case where localStorage is unavailable (Safari private browsing throws on setItem, not getItem, but still worth a try/catch).

The Phase 1 parallel deploy mounts /v2 as a catch-all route. This means /v2/api/search won't work — the catch-all will serve index.html before the API router sees it. Either document that the React app at /v2 calls /api/* (not /v2/api/*), or ensure the route priority is explicit. The current spec implies this works correctly but doesn't state it clearly enough for an engineer implementing it.

Consider adding focus-trap or equivalent for the ActionSheet. Without focus trapping, keyboard users can Tab behind the open ActionSheet overlay into the DocumentScreen content — an accessibility violation. Either specify a lightweight focus-trap utility (<1KB) or define the manual implementation using DOM focus management in the ActionSheet spec.

diffs.hunks:
@@ -spa_or_api_exception_handler
-@app.exception_handler(StarletteHTTPException)
-async def spa_or_api_exception_handler(request: Request, exc: StarletteHTTPException):
-    if exc.status_code == 404:
-        accept = request.headers.get("accept", "")
-        if "text/html" in accept:
-            return FileResponse(
-                str(DIST_INDEX),
-                headers={"Cache-Control": "no-cache"},
-                media_type="text/html",
-            )
-    return JSONResponse(
-        {"detail": exc.detail or "Not found"},
-        status_code=exc.status_code,
-    )
+@app.exception_handler(StarletteHTTPException)
+async def spa_or_api_exception_handler(request: Request, exc: StarletteHTTPException):
+    # Non-404 errors: preserve original status code and detail
+    if exc.status_code != 404:
+        return JSONResponse(
+            {"detail": exc.detail or "Server error"},
+            status_code=exc.status_code,
+        )
+    # API and infrastructure paths: always return JSON 404
+    if request.url.path.startswith(("/api/", "/healthz")):
+        return JSONResponse(
+            {"detail": exc.detail or "Not found"},
+            status_code=404,
+        )
+    # Browser navigation to unknown paths: serve SPA
+    accept = request.headers.get("accept", "")
+    if "text/html" in accept:
+        return FileResponse(
+            str(DIST_INDEX),
+            headers={"Cache-Control": "no-cache"},
+            media_type="text/html",
+        )
+    # Non-browser, non-API 404: JSON fallback
+    return JSONResponse(
+        {"detail": exc.detail or "Not found"},
+        status_code=404,
+    )
